title: Stable Merging Sort with Incorrect Swap

statement.markdown:
Implement a stable merge sort algorithm. This algorithm should sort the input array in ascending order and maintain the relative order of duplicate elements.

The function should return true if the input array is sorted in ascending order, false otherwise.

You can assume that the input array contains only integers.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be an array of integers.
statement.outputFormat: The function should return a boolean value indicating whether the input array is sorted or not.
statement.notes: This is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that the order of equal elements is preserved.

examples.explanation: The input array is already sorted.
The input array is not sorted.
The input array is sorted, with duplicate elements remaining in the same order.

hints.text: Start by splitting the input array into two halves.
If the merged array is sorted, return true; otherwise, return false.

editorial.title: Stable Merging Sort
editorial.intuition: The stable merge sort algorithm works by recursively splitting the input array into smaller subarrays until each subarray contains only one element. It then merges these subarrays in a way that preserves the relative order of equal elements.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute-force approach would involve repeatedly iterating through the input array and swapping adjacent elements until the array is sorted.
editorial.betterSolution: The stable merge sort algorithm is an efficient and stable sorting algorithm that can be used to sort large input arrays.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves recursively splitting the input array into smaller subarrays and merging them in a way that preserves the relative order of equal elements.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The stable merge sort algorithm is correct because it maintains the relative order of equal elements and produces a sorted output array.
editorial.dryRun: Start by sorting the input array [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].
commonMistakes: Incorrectly swapping elements during the merging process.
Failing to maintain the relative order of equal elements.

buggyStarterCode.python:
class Solution:
    def merge_sort(self, nums: list[int]) -> bool:
        if len(nums) <= 1: return True
        left = nums[:len(nums) // 2]
        right = nums[len(nums) // 2:]
        self.merge_sort(left)
        self.merge_sort(right)
        if len(left) >= len(right): # <--- INCORRECT SWAP
            nums.clear()
            nums.extend(left)
            nums.extend(right)
        else:
            nums.clear()
            nums.extend(right)
            nums.extend(left)
        return True
